sentences.text: To tell you the honest truth, I-- I really don't know if it was a week or if it was four days or three days.
I don't know.
The only thing that I had forget to mention, that all those months that we were in Auschwitz, we cried so much that our eyes just dried up.
It got to the point when no tears were coming out.
That we used up all our tears.
And all the tears that we had shed while we were in Auschwitz, a whole river could form.
That's how much.
And a lot of things that I forget to mention, that what was going on during the night, that how the people were crying and singing and all that.

But once we were leaving and we were at the gate and I was at the front, I was thinking there is such a thing that we will walk out of here, and not the way the-- Lola, the block-- the Lageralteste had said that we would go through the smoke.
So I said, then it must be a miracle.
After all this hiding of the selection, going from one barrack to the next, and then trying to finagle here, finagle there.
So I said, maybe God is really good to us.
So we start go.
We were supposed to be 550 girls.
But by the time they get wind of it, that we are going, there was at least 15,000.
So they cut it down, and I was happy I was at the front of the line, that I wasn't cut down.
As we were marched and we were going, we were passing between the same two lager, the men's lager and the [GERMAN], as we were both in.
And we are going.
And as we are going, we were taken into a crematorium, because what happened is that during-- what I forget to mention, which is very important.
We were taken in to sanitize a few times during our stay.
And we got different clothing.
That's how when I arrived, I got the black clothes, black dress.
When I was selected from my younger sister and the [GERMAN] grab my arm, that was after another how you call them, sterilization or cleaning, whatever it is.
De-licing, yeah.
That was the name of it.
So once we were going to be de-liced to-- back to Auschwitz, the town itself.
And as we were going, there was a building, a brick building, far away.
And there was a man who was hollering to us.
Is anybody from [HUNGARIAN].
So I said to him, are you Meier?
I said, tell-- I said, we are-- we are from Mandok, because my grandparents lived there.
So I know him.
So I said, have you seen my father, or somebody?
He said, oh, yes.
Your father and your brother was here.
They-- they should be back soon.
I said, please tell him and my brother, Philip, that we are the three of us here, Clara, Magda, and Elizabeth.
And our cousin [HUNGARIAN].
That we are here and coming way back, maybe we could meet them or we could we see them.
And I said, you two sisters, [HUNGARIAN] and [HUNGARIAN], I said they are in the [GERMAN].
So he was happy to hear it.
He said, do you know from anybody else?
And meantime, the [GERMAN] came over and gave me such a bang on my face and my neck that I didn't know how-- how to-- how to survive it.
But I said, it was worth it.
At least I heard the news and I know.
It was like a lift.
So we were taken into this Auschwitz town.
And there was a German soldier who had like a pump and he was pumping.
You had to spread your leg.
And your head.
And then he gave the pump to one of the half-lings, and she was doing it.
So when we were going to-- to-- to be taken away, I had seen that we-- we are taken into the crematorium.
Because we were passing it.
And the crematoriums had blankets on the fence, that you couldn't see it in.
You couldn't see through.
When the gate opened and I seen it, I looked at my sister Clara.
And I said, for this we have to suffer all this time.
For this we have to wait, that we wind up in the end here.
There were glasses on the entrance as you went in.
There were shoes, children's shoes, in the other end.
And there was like suitcases, things like that in the other side and so on.
And I said, this, I don't want to believe it.
I don't want to believe it, that this is what happened.
And as I'm complaining, there was a German [GERMAN] who came with the bicycle out of breath.
And she said, no, no, no.
These people are not for here.
These people are not for here.
These people is for the-- for the other place, where they shower them and they clean them.
So we turn around, the whole group was turned around.
And I-- we were the last one on the line.
So I said it's no more things like that.
So the gate closed after us, and we went into another building and they showered us.
They took away our dress.
And as we were coming out, that's where they registered us.
That's where they gave us the-- they ask for our name.
They ask who came in with us, how many people came in with, the immediate family.
And who we-- who are we.
And then they gave us a-- a-- as we were going out later on, as we were standing in line, then they were gaving us a white piece of cloth with the numbers on it.
I don't know it was 55978, or 55-- but--
No.
Because you see, when we were taken into the [GERMAN], we were-- they call it the special name, the-- that [GERMAN].

Because from the [GERMAN], if there were people-- let's say a transport came in and it wasn't enough people to-- to use up the-- the poison for the crematorium, that they took a few thousand from there.
If they needed for worker, they took it from there.
So really, these people weren't-- weren't meant to live, who were at the [GERMAN].
So we never had the-- the tattoo on our arm.
But when we were leaving and they-- they ask us our name, and that's when they gave the number.
So we were put up on the train wagon, the cattle car. 40 of us.
So you could dance.
That's how much room was there, comparing when we were taken into the place, 80 with the bundles and everything.
So before we got on the train, they gave us a loaf of bread.
And they gave us a clean coat and a dress.
And I think they even gave us a petticoat, but I'm not sure.
Did you have shoes?
Well, the shoes I had what I came in from home when they-- they started to deport us.
And as we were lining up to get on the train, there was this little skinny woman like a rat.
And she was saying in Yiddish that give me your bread.
I give you a comb.
Give me your bread.
You don't need the bread.
You're going taken to the crematorium.
You don't need the bread.
So I said to her in Hungarian, I said, no.
I need the bread.
I don't need the comb.
I have no hair.
I mean, I don't need the comb.
So we went up on the train, 40 of us.
And there was two German-- and-- soldier who was putting with us the same system.
One bucket with the water.
One bucket for the extreme.
And came the time when he sat down, and he was eating his dinner or whatever it was.
A slab of bacon and the bread.
So one of the girl, this [HUNGARIAN] girl, says to him in German-- because he-- she spoke Jewish-- those people from under the carpet area, they all spoke Jewish.
That was their mother language, whereas for us, the Hungarian unfortunately.
So she says to the soldier, if you give me the skin of the-- the slab of bacon, that I will be nice to you.
No.
She said give me the-- the skin.
So the man said-- the soldier said, I give you the skin if you will be nice to me tonight.
So she said, oh, yes, yes, yes.
And the other girls were telling her, you making a very big mistake.
You selling your body for that piece of skin.
She said, I'm not going to give him my body.
In Hungarian, she was saying.
She said, I was just telling him.
So they say, yeah, you're telling.
But comes the nighttime, you will have to perform.
And then he gave her the skin.
And she was happy, and she was carrying in a conversation with him like it was one of the best social hall.
Came nighttime, she disappeared among us.
She was hiding.
And I feel, as I was sitting-- because I was sitting not far.
I was sitting on this side of the-- the-- the cattle wagon.
And that took place at the other side where the door was.
So I didn't pay any attention.
During the night because I'm sitting there because we were all five of us lined up and this the way we sit.
And slept.
I hear-- feel a hairy hand on my leg.
So I picked it up and I pushed it away.
Then my sister Elizabeth said-- she says, what's the matter with you?
What do you want from me?
You know.
I said, I don't bother you.
I said, he bothered me too.
I said just pinch the hand or just push it away.
I said, don't let it--
Then a few minutes later, I hear cursing in German.
And he picked up the bucket shit and he throw it at us from his anger.
So we weren't angry at him.
We were angry at the girl that-- that she got us into such a position, that he finally-- we had a glean-- a-- a clean clothes.
Finally we were washed.
And now we had to have the-- the smell on us.
It was terrible.
But terrible because we wish for clean things.
So as we are going, the same thing whenever the train stopped, they gave us clean water.
Somebody had to take down the bucket, empty it.
We were going through a [INAUDIBLE] place.
And there was this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] master of the-- the train track, or station.
There is a name for it.
I can't remember now.
And he had bribed these German soldiers that they should able to hand in a pot full of potatoes, cooked potatoes.
They were giving cigarettes to these soldiers to let the potatoes up on the-- the wagon.
So when we got the potatoes, but they said they want the bucket back.
And that they want it empty.
So you have nothing there.
Where should you put it?
So we put it on the board, on the train floor.
And we flattened it down with our hand, and we made a nice square shape.
And with my knife, we arranged it 40 pieces.
So when a girl picked up a piece and it was like seven heaven.
It had fried onion in it.
And just when she picked it up and she put it to her nose and she was smelling it at least for five minutes before she put it in her mouth, as hungry as she was.
So the meantime, I had a woman there with me who wanted to cut the bread to make five, you know, four portion out of it.
So I said to her, I give you my knife to cut the bread.
But cut out the middle slice, a small slice, for the knife, for the use of the knife.
She kept on cursing me, but cursing me.
I said then forget about it.
I don't want your bread.
Don't take my knife.
Forget about it.
And again she comes to me.
And she looked like a real gypsy, you know.
Black complexion, and the little black hair.
And I really didn't like her.
If I liked her, I would give her the knife.
But I didn't like her because she was using such a vulgar language that it was sickening.
And cursing.
And she wouldn't agree to give the bread.
And I was getting tired of it already, hearing her cursing and cursing.
So I said to her, here is the knife, and you should choke on the piece of bread that you-- you were supposed to give me.
So she gave me back the knife.
We were going.
Finally, after I don't know how many days, but it was quite a long while, we got to a train station.
We stopped.
The door opens.
And there were some new German soldiers who took us over.
And as we are marching, we see that this is a big, big place.
The lights and civilization.
As we going-- walking towards the track because there was a tremendous wide track, train track.
We are walking and walking.
And then we said to each other, this lousy so-and-so Germans, they making a fool out of us.
They tell us they going to take us to work, and here we are marching, marching, so we going to wind up in another crematorium and they going to finish us.
One of these German soldier who was walking by, he said in perfect Hungarian, he said, don't be afraid, girls.
You're not going to be cremated.
You will be working.
So when we heard that, we were even more scared because here he overheard our conversation what we are saying.
So we kept quiet.
We didn't say anything more.
Finally, we get into a beautiful place with beautiful new barrack.
Each individual bed with blanket on it.
And the individual little plate with a spoon.
We said, this is heaven.
I mean, is such a thing exist?
And we get a towel, a cloth little towel.
By the way, we did get a soap in Auschwitz, a little gray block of soap once when we at the delousing place.
And they told us that this is from your family, that they made the soap out of it.
It was gray, and it had no bubble to it, nothing.
No smell.
Just-- that-- that came to my mind now that we are in Nuremberg.
And we said that this place is like heaven.
Because as we were going, we had seen at the train station that Nuremberg.
That's how we know it.
Otherwise-- they didn't tell us that we take you to Nuremberg.
So we-- and like I said, when we went in, this place, this barrack, this lager, was opposite a cemetery.
As we were coming from the train station, on the right side was the lager.
On the left side was a tremendous, tremendous big cemetery.
So what happened is we were there.
And we thought we are in seven heaven.
One person in a bed with a blanket?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got such a delicious food that day.
It had potatoes in it.
It was thick and it was a big, big plate full.
And the German [GERMAN] was the one who was portioning out the food.
And I had seen that after she had scooped up the food, that she put the [HUNGARIAN] outside.
And I saw there was a lot of food left in it.
So when it quieted down, I went out and I started to lick the [HUNGARIAN] with my hand, and it was delicious.
No sooner I was enjoying myself, this big husky German woman comes along.
She was hitting me and hitting me and kicking me.
I fainted.
When I woke up, my nose was bleeding.
My tooth was loose.
I go in.
I could hardly creep into the room to go up on the bed.
I said, this lousy so-and-so.
They rather wash it out than-- than a person should-- should eat it up.
So that's how the introduction was to Nuremberg.
Then later on, about three or four days later, an alarm was, and we were taken into a shelter which was underground in a cement seat, a long cement seat.
We were seated there.
By the time we came up from the shelter, there were no more four barracks.
There were only two left.
It just burned them to ashes.
Well, there must have been an air raid, because the Germans shipped us to the-- to the shelter.
So when we came up, there was no more-- no more-- how you call them.
No more barracks.
Then later on, they were selecting that they need people to go to work.
So there was a German [GERMAN] who took us.
We were 60 of us.
We went with the trolley car.
The last trolley car, the last portion of the trolley car, was ours.
We weren't supposed to mingle with the civilian.
So we went to work.
And when the gate opened, we marched in.
They were civilians there.
We were taken upstairs to the second floor or something, and we were given a lecture that you going to do this and this and this.
And if you sabotage, than when we test the finished product, it will blow you up.
Because that's the way it will be.
So we will lock you into a special cabin, and that's where the testing will take place.
So actually, none of us wanted to sabotage because we know that we will be blown up with that.
So the work consisted of all different wires, like electric wires.
Yellow, green, blue, red.
And we had to pull it through.
There was a big frame like a wrought iron frame.
And in that frame, there were different little holes, and we had to pull different colors in different place and so on.
And then when it got to the end, then you had to-- had a special screw, and you had to tie it up.
And there was a special cover that you had to cover it up.
But each one had different assignment.
Like one who was pulling the wire through.
The other one who was putting the top on it.
And the other one who was doing the covering.
At one occasion, there was a alarm.
And they took us down to the bunker, to the shelter.
And in that shelter, there was all kinds of heavy pulleys, like they are pulling up big heavy load on a ship before the-- the-- when they loading up the ship.
It was hanging all on the wall.
And as we are staying there, the 60 of us, the two German [GERMAN] stayed with us.
They came down with us.
We hear then the bomb is shaking, and the bomb hit the top of us.
We know that sooner or later, we going to choke.
Because the whole thing going to collapse.
So when it quieted down a little, the [GERMAN] says that when we came in, there was a little teeny window.
So the skinniest girl should come over and we will clean the debris from the window and climb through, and go and ask for help.
But you have to speak German or some other language, and so on.
So luckily, this little skinny girl, she climbed through the window and she went and there were French and Italian prisoners who were working there.
And then she told them that come and help to clean the doorway so that these girls could come out.
They said, oh, there must be Germans and they send you.
We don't-- we not going to rescue no Germans.
So she said, no.
These are all my kind.
These are all half-lings.
So they came, and they made a little opening at the door.
We walked out.
The minute we walked out, the 60 of us, the whole building collapsed.
When-- when somebody wanted to-- the [GERMAN] said go back and get the [HUNGARIAN].
There were no more-- no more where to go.
Then we started to march home--
Yeah.
It was the Simmons factory.
The Simmons factory.
So then as we were going home, the electric wires were all over on the place.
You had to watch it that you shouldn't step on the live wire.

When we get to near to the railway station, the bridge that was the overpass was holes on it that you didn't know if you going to fell into it or you were able to go on that teeny little narrow ledge that was left over.
So we managed.
But that was a-- a nightmare, how we got back to our lager.
When we get back to our lager my sister Elizabeth and-- and my cousin, their eyes is cried out because they were told that we were bombed down, and goodbye sisters.
No more sisters.
So I said to her, thank God we are back and so on.
Meantime in the big commotion because they were bombing on the lager also, she managed to steal a small little [HUNGARIAN] with all beautiful delicious potato soup.
So we started eating.
And naturally there are other girls.
They start to come over.
It's no-- no privacy.
You can't do too much.
So we gave some of them-- some of them.
We were sick.
Our stomach was so puffed up, so full.
Meantime when it came Sunday, Sunday they didn't take us to work.
So we had a chance to relax or some time.
Before Christmas, the Blockalteste and the Lageralteste were teaching the girls how to perform, how to sing different German songs to entertain the German crew.
Meantime the Lageralteste befriended the Oberscharfuehrer, and they became very good friends.
Now, there was another [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] woman soldier, the-- I can't think of the name of it now.
She was in love with the Oberscharfuehrer.
And she was jealous of Lola.
So there was a very big rivalry.
When the-- the Oberscharfuehrer had to be-- go-- had to be sent away for something, had to attend some kind of a meeting or something, then the-- the-- this German woman, she was the head.
So she made us kneel in the snow, in the ice, all day long to-- to get her frustration out.
At one occasion, I was able to sneak near to the kitchen, and I bend in through the window and I picked up one of these leek.
You know, that has the long green thing.
So we ate it.
We almost died from it.
That was too much for our stomach.
Even the four of us ate it.
We made a resolution never again, because this was a-- a miracle that it didn't kill us.
Yes.
There, we had a-- there we had a wash room where we had more water, and being that the Lageralteste had access to hot water to bathe in, all of that.
So from that hot water, we were able to wash ourself sometimes.
Not always, but sometime.
And then as the bombing continued, then it was no more hot water.
It was only cold water.
So what happened is that the-- the food wasn't as plentiful as it was before.
They were giving us beautiful bread, the long-- it was like I would say a kilo.
And we cut it in four, and each one of us had one portion.
But it was good bread.
It wasn't the sawdust, what we had in Auschwitz.
And one day, a truck came in who delivered the bread.
So naturally, I wasn't anxious to deliver the bread inside.
I was anxious to see how can I get the bread to disappear.
So the truck driver was such a sweet, nice guy even though he was a German soldier.
I took the bread and I made believe that I'm giving it to the other guy.
Meantime I made it disappear between my leg.
And as I told you before that in Auschwitz, we got this coat.
So I get a coat that was like a rain coat.
And this rain coat had a pocket that had a slit that you could put things inside into the lining.
But the meantime, the pocket was there.
So I put the bread between my leg, and through my pocket I was holding the bread.
So I went over to the [GERMAN], yeah.
Before I couldn't think of the name of it.
I told her in German-- I learned that my German-- that I have to go to the bathroom.
So she gave me a push, so I went.
When I went into the barrack, I told my sister, hold the bread.
Maybe I could get another one.
So she put it under her dress, and she was sitting on top of the bed.
So I couldn't get another bread because by that time, we pushed the truck out from the mud and went away.
There was one occasion when they-- meantime, they selected my sister Elizabeth and my cousin, they were working inside in the-- in the lager.
And they were doing small little spinning and small-- like a battery thickness.
And they were putting on little wires like-- like a thread.
They were doing that.
Because they selected all the girls who had fine fingers.
The only thing is they had two groups over there.
One was working daytime.
One was working nighttime.
And my sister Elizabeth and my cousin was selected to work at nighttime.
OK.
